quote: you know, I was just interested. I was trying to find out before I go any further. How much is it gonna cost me in in the end?
stewart_read: Bobby stated his gating condition in plain language: cost is the unlock. Carter deflected correctly (no fabricated price) but missed the flip entirely. The schema move here is to redirect Bobby's cost anxiety back at his utility bill — 'what you're already paying is the cost we're trying to replace.' Carter instead described the appointment format, which gave Bobby no emotional reason to commit. This is the earliest leverage point in a multi-failure call.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Carter was deliberately withholding the flip to preserve it for the appointment. Rejected — Carter showed no evidence of a setup move, and Bobby never reached the appointment.
coaching_implication: Run the objection_inversion drill with Carter on cost objections. The flip doesn't require a number — it requires redirecting the customer's cost anxiety toward the utility bill they're already paying. Suggested rephrase intent: 'The cost question is exactly why we do the appointment — right now you're paying [utility] every month regardless. The appointment shows whether solar replaces that or not.' No fabricated savings figure needed.

quote: I know you can sell the credits.
stewart_read: Bobby volunteered prior knowledge of solar credits unprompted — this is not a blank-slate skeptic. He has done some research. Carter acknowledged with 'Yeah' and moved to a financial claim rather than expanding on Bobby's existing interest. A top setter uses this as the entry point into verify.interest_in_solar: 'What got you looking into it?' would have opened the emotional anchor Carter never found.
coaching_implication: Coach Carter to treat unsolicited customer knowledge as an invitation to go deeper, not a box to check. When a customer volunteers awareness, the move is a curiosity question — 'What got you looking into it?' — not a pivot to features or financials.

quote: No. It's not. I'm I'm on a call. I'm a plumber, and I got a emergency call for a house in Inwood, to get the water back on. She's having a party tonight.
stewart_read: Bobby disclosed a genuine job emergency — mid-call, mid-job, someone's water is off. Carter's response was 'Okay. So let me actually do this' and moved immediately to logistics. Yielding the call was correct; Carter should not push a customer mid-emergency. The failure was what happened next: Carter accepted Bobby's unstructured 'email me in a week' without locking any callback time. The empathy miss is not that Carter yielded — it's that he didn't acknowledge the situation humanly before releasing, and he left with no mutual commitment.
alternative_hypothesis: Reactive read: Carter should have pushed harder to keep Bobby on the call. Rejected — pushing mid-emergency is the wrong move and would have damaged rapport. The failure is the absence of a locked callback time, not the decision to yield.
coaching_implication: Teach Carter the two-part emergency release: (1) brief human acknowledgment ('Go handle it — sounds urgent'), then (2) a locked callback before hanging up ('When's a good time tomorrow — morning or afternoon?'). The acknowledgment costs five seconds and earns goodwill; the locked time is the difference between a recoverable lead and a cold email.

quote: I appreciate it. Of course. And what I did, I shot you a text. So you have my personal number and personal bank number, and then I'll send you my email over text as well.
stewart_read: Carter closed by handing Bobby his personal contact info and promising to send his email — no locked time, no mutual commitment, no verify data collected. The follow-up obligation is entirely Carter's. This is a soft outcome with no structural anchor: Bobby said 'I'll email you in a week' and Carter accepted it. The 'personal bank number' phrase in the transcript is also anomalous and should be flagged for audio verification (see coaching implication).
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Carter gave personal contact info to build trust with a skeptical customer. Partially valid — the micro-trade instinct is right. But a personal number without a locked time is a trust gesture that doesn't convert to a sit.
coaching_implication: Two items for the manager: (1) Pull the audio at 03:26 and verify what Bobby actually said — 'personal bank number' is either a transcript error or a compliance issue that needs immediate review. (2) Coach Carter that personal contact info is a trust tool, not a close. The move is: personal number + locked callback time. Without the time anchor, the personal number just means Carter is waiting on Bobby's schedule indefinitely.

quote: There's not a very big house. I mean, it's, 2,000 square foot.
stewart_read: Bobby volunteered his home size unprompted — a direct verify entry point Carter did not use. A top setter pivots here: 'Good to know — and is that a single story or two? What's the roof like?' This opens address/homeowner confirmation, roof type/age, and residence context in one natural exchange. Carter acknowledged with 'Okay' and moved on.
coaching_implication: Show Carter how a volunteered home detail is a verify door opening. When a customer gives you a data point, the move is a follow-on question that advances qualification — not a neutral acknowledgment. Bobby gave Carter two free verify entries (home size, implicit homeownership) that Carter left on the table.

quote: Does the the government credit, does that pay for a lot of it?
stewart_read: Bobby asked a second unsolicited question about the tax credit — after disclosing a job emergency and asking to be called back. This is a warmer signal than the call's trajectory suggests: a customer who is truly disengaged doesn't ask follow-up questions mid-exit. Carter's response ('Yeah. I can help out quite a bit') was vague and missed the opportunity to use this re-engagement moment to lock a callback time. Bobby's warmest line ('something to think about') came immediately after this exchange.
coaching_implication: Coach Carter to recognize re-engagement signals even in exit sequences. When a customer asks a question on the way out the door, that's the moment to attempt a soft rebook: 'Glad you asked — that's exactly what the specialist walks through. Can we lock in a quick call tomorrow so you get the real numbers?' Bobby's curiosity was live at 03:38; Carter let it expire.

quote: what I do know is with the tax incentives, you're paying nothing out of pocket to get started.
stewart_read: Carter made a financial promise — 'paying nothing out of pocket to get started' — in response to Bobby's cost question. The schema explicitly notes that Ion can no longer guarantee the tax credit on purchased systems. Carter's claim is either outdated or overstated, and it creates a promise the closer will have to walk back. This is scope creep into closer-tier financial content, compounded by a potentially inaccurate claim.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Carter used the tax credit as a curiosity hook to keep Bobby engaged. Partially valid as intent — but the execution introduced a financial promise that may not hold, which is a closer-tier liability, not a setter-tier tool.
coaching_implication: Carter needs to know the current tax credit disclosure language from the schema: Ion can no longer guarantee the credit on purchased systems. The setter's job is to create curiosity about the appointment, not to make financial promises. Suggested rephrase intent: 'There are incentives that can bring the cost down significantly — the specialist will show you exactly what applies to your situation.' No promise, same curiosity hook.
